briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. We will now begin the climate briefing for March 12, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Climate Crisis and Global Warnings
sections.content: News collected today once again underscores the severity of global climate change and the worldwide warnings it entails. Domestically, the estimated cost of global damage per ton of greenhouse gas has been calculated at 280,000 KRW. The climate crisis is becoming an everyday reality, with large-scale wildfires and a scarcity of fruit tree saplings due to climate change causing difficulties for farmers, leading to calls for urgent establishment of legal frameworks [3, 4, 5]. Internationally, overheating homes in London, intensifying giant hail and destructive tornadoes in Illinois, and heatwaves in Los Angeles are being identified as direct impacts of climate change, sparking discussions [112, 113, 121, 136]. Notably, record-breaking floods in Brazilian coffee-producing regions have prompted scientists to warn of the necessity to cease fossil fuel use [138]. Furthermore, changes in global systems are being detected, such as the reduction in Arctic sea ice altering the formula for cloud 'seed' emissions and slowing Earth's rotation, leading to an unprecedented increase in day length for the first time in 3.6 million years [17, 119]. Alongside these developments, concerns have also been raised that the Winter Olympics are threatened by climate change [118].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Strengthening Domestic Climate Policies and Legal Frameworks
sections.content: Efforts to strengthen domestic policies and legal frameworks for climate crisis response are accelerating. To expedite greenhouse gas reduction, the government is advancing the follow-up plan for climate change response technologies by one year, with the second basic plan for climate change response technology development scheduled for announcement this September [2, 16]. The Ministry of Science and ICT has launched a Climate Change Response Promotion Committee to explore solutions for achieving the National Greenhouse Gas Reduction Target (NDC), while the Ministry of Climate has laid the groundwork for legal responses, including preparing a government proposal for a 'Special Act on Climate Crisis Adaptation' [18, 30]. Notably, the 'Special Act on Green Shipping Routes' has passed the National Assembly, signaling the full-scale transition to carbon neutrality in the shipping sector and the establishment of eco-friendly routes [39]. The National Assembly's Special Committee on Climate Crisis has commenced deliberation on 18 climate-related bills, including the Carbon Neutrality Act, thereby accelerating legislative discussions [52]. President Lee also emphasized the unwavering pursuit of a rapid transition centered on renewable energy and the restructuring of the petrochemical industry [99].

sections.title: 🌱 Technological Innovation and Community Efforts for Carbon Neutrality Transition
sections.content: Technological innovation and concrete community efforts for the transition to a carbon-neutral society are actively underway. The Korea Institute of Ocean Science and Technology (KIOST) is proactively responding to the climate crisis by advancing marine climate prediction R&D and establishing an AI-based precision prediction platform for typhoons and marine conditions [1, 20, 23, 27]. Domestic companies are achieving 'Leadership (A-)' ratings or awards in CDP climate change assessments, recognizing their performance in climate change and water resource management, with LG Innotek securing a 'Leadership A' rating for three consecutive years [14, 29, 132]. Furthermore, the Korea Environment Corporation and OhooDooSi Lab are collaborating in a public-private partnership to develop an AI-based carbon emission management system, supporting carbon management for both public and private sectors [56, 69, 76]. Meanwhile, the Jeonnam Provincial Office of Education is expanding ecological transition education by selecting 50 carbon-neutral leading schools for 2026 [31, 32, 33, 40, 41, 51, 54, 62, 63, 65, 66, 67, 70, 73, 74]. Various local governments, including Yangsan City, Chungju City, and Jinju City, are spearheading the realization of carbon neutrality in their communities through initiatives such as declaring a 2030 Green City Vision, launching carbon neutrality action teams, accelerating the supply of electric and hydrogen vehicles, and distributing eco-friendly vans [38, 46, 47, 48, 72, 78].

sections.title: 🌱 Expanding Renewable Energy and Strengthening Energy Security
sections.content: The expansion of renewable energy is emerging as a key task for strengthening energy security and achieving carbon neutrality goals. Local government efforts are active, with Jeonnam Province fully embarking on the creation of public-led renewable energy complexes, and Incheon City promoting the supply of new and renewable energy tailored to local characteristics and the establishment of a sustainable future energy ecosystem [79, 85]. In response to the energy crisis originating from the Middle East, the government and ruling party are accelerating legislation for renewable energy expansion alongside the early operation of nuclear power plants and promoting voucher support for vulnerable groups [93, 95, 96, 98, 105, 107, 110]. Technological advancements are also continuing, with LS ELECTRIC leveraging AI to predict renewable energy generation and accelerating its power brokerage business, and Hyosung Heavy Industries showing strong performance in its ESS business [90, 109]. Market interest is also growing, with nuclear, solar, and new and renewable energy ETFs gaining prominence due to surging oil prices [83, 84, 102, 111]. However, institutional and industrial challenges also exist, such as criticisms that the proposed RPS (Renewable Portfolio Standard) reform is insufficient to meet the government's goal of tripling renewable energy by 2030, and the dominance of Chinese products in the solar market [80, 87, 103].

sections.title: 📰 Promoting Climate Change Awareness and International Cooperation
sections.content: Public awareness of climate change and discussions on international cooperation are also steadily continuing. Wando Arboretum is hosting a special exhibition on climate change titled 'The Art of Survival,' highlighting the diverse survival strategies of organisms amidst the climate crisis and drawing public attention [6, 9, 12, 21, 22, 24, 26, 28]. Choi Jae-chul, Director of the Climate Change Center, is contributing to fostering awareness among future generations by delivering special lectures at universities, emphasizing the importance of green transition in the era of climate crisis [7, 10, 19, 25]. Internationally, the Korea Energy Agency is inviting applications for new Korea-developing country cooperation projects for climate change response in 2026, and a Cornell alumnus is lecturing on the influence of Small Island Developing States (SIDS) in shaping international climate change law, indicating active legal and policy discussions [11, 15, 120]. Various international issues are also being addressed, including Iran's water problems, the progress of deep-sea mining treaties, and women farmers and climate change [115]. Interestingly, King Penguins appear to be exceptionally benefiting from climate change, but scientists are still urging a cautious approach [114, 128, 134].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has once again highlighted the severe warnings of climate change, along with diverse domestic and international response efforts, particularly emphasizing the importance of carbon-neutral technology development and renewable energy transition. Recognizing both these crises and opportunities, we must not cease our progress towards a sustainable future. This concludes today's climate briefing.
